w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: • Flow: Introduce → Assess → Stretch → Re-assess → Document

"I'm going to measure the length of the muscles on the back of your thigh, then perform a stretch to help improve that length. I'll measure before and after using this tool. Let me know if you feel any pain."
Palpate each landmark and SAY OUT LOUD:
"I'm identifying my landmarks — here is the greater trochanter, here is the lateral midline of the pelvis, and here is the lateral epicondyle."

"Lie on your back with both legs straight and flat. I'm going to keep your knee straight and slowly lift one leg up until I feel tightness in the back of your thigh. Just relax and let me do the work."
Hold in FULL EXTENSION — firm pressure on anterior thigh
Stays FLAT on the table to prevent posterior pelvic tilt
Bend that knee, foot flat on table to neutralize pelvis
Stop when you feel resistance in posterior thigh AND further flexion would cause knee flexion, posterior pelvic tilt, or lumbar flexion.
Firm — tension in semimembranosus, semitendinosus, and biceps femoris.
"I'm stabilizing the knee in full extension and keeping the opposite leg flat to prevent the pelvis from tilting."
Lateral aspect of the hip joint — at the greater trochanter
Along the lateral midline of the pelvis
Along the lateral midline of the femur — toward the lateral epicondyle
"Your hamstring length measures ___ degrees of hip flexion.
is 68 to 80 degrees with the knee held in extension."
After the SLR measurement, FLEX THE KNEE and attempt more hip flexion.
Hip flexion INCREASES with knee bent

"Now I'm bending the knee to take tension off the two-joint hamstrings. If I get more hip motion with the knee bent, that confirms the hamstrings are limiting — not a one-joint structure like the glute max or the joint capsule."
Lift the straight leg into hip flexion to end range.
One hand behind ankle/calf, other hand keeping knee in full extension.
comfortable and secure on the table.
HIGHEST VALUE ITEM — 6 points. Monitor the ENTIRE time for every rep.
on the stretch leg for entire hold
"I'm maintaining the knee in extension and keeping the pelvis stable throughout the entire stretch."
"Do you feel a stretch in the back of your thigh?"
If they say behind the knee:
May be nerve or posterior capsule — back off slightly.
"I'm going to hold this stretch for about 60 seconds. We'll repeat
Re-do the EXACT same SLR test with the same goniometer setup.
"Now I'm re-measuring. Your hamstring length now measures ___ degrees. That's an improvement of ___ degrees compared to before."

"I would document: Performed hamstring muscle length assessment using SLR test. Initial: ___ degrees hip flexion with knee extended. Intervention: manual static hamstring stretch in supine, 3 reps x 60-second holds at moderate intensity. Post-intervention: ___ degrees. Patient tolerated well with improved hamstring length."
"Now I'm going to measure the length of your hip flexor muscle on the front of your hip, then perform a stretch. I'll measure before and after. Let me know if anything is painful."
Palpate each landmark and SAY OUT LOUD:
"I'm identifying my landmarks — here is the greater trochanter, here is the lateral midline of the pelvis, and here is the lateral epicondyle."

"Sit at the very end of the table. I'm going to help you lie back while you hold both knees to your chest. Then I'll slowly lower one leg down toward the table. Just relax that leg and let gravity bring it down."
Patient holds it TO CHEST — locks pelvis in posterior tilt, flattens lumbar spine
Use a towel or sheet behind the knee
Test leg stays in sagittal plane:
"Keep holding this knee pulled tight to your chest — that keeps your low back flat and pelvis stable. Now relax this other leg completely and let gravity do the work."
Lateral aspect of the hip joint — at the greater trochanter
Along the lateral midline of the pelvis
Along the lateral midline of the femur — toward the lateral epicondyle
"Your hip flexor length measures ___ degrees. Ideally the thigh should rest flat on the table — right now it's sitting ___ degrees above, indicating some shortness."
If the thigh does NOT reach the table, REPEAT the test with the knee held in extension.
NORMAL — both iliopsoas & rectus femoris normal
Thigh OFF table, knee fully extended
"I'm straightening the knee to take tension off the two-joint
femoris. If the thigh drops to the table now, the rectus femoris is tight. If it still doesn't reach, the iliopsoas — which is a one-joint hip flexor — is the restricting muscle."
Patient at end of table, holding opposite knee to chest.
on the distal anterior thigh to increase stretch.
and patient feels secure on the table.
HIGHEST VALUE ITEM — 6 points. Monitor the ENTIRE time for every rep.
the ENTIRE hold — this stabilizes the pelvis
Pelvis tilts anteriorly → you LOSE the stretch
COMMON ERROR: Patient gradually releases opposite knee during hold.
nice and tight. I need your low back to stay flat against the table the whole time. I'm maintaining stabilization throughout."
"Do you feel a stretch in the front of your hip?"
Lumbar spine extending — re-stabilize, pull opposite knee tighter.
May be rectus femoris/patellar tendon — adjust knee position.
Could be adductors — check for hip rotation or abduction.
"I'm going to hold this for about 60 seconds. We'll do
Re-do the EXACT same Thomas test with the same goniometer setup.
"After stretching, your hip flexor length now measures ___ degrees. That's an improvement of ___ degrees."

"I would document: Performed iliopsoas muscle length assessment using Modified Thomas Test. Initial: ___ degrees hip extension. Intervention: manual static iliopsoas stretch in Thomas test position, 3 reps x 60-second holds at moderate intensity. Post-intervention: ___ degrees. Patient tolerated well with improved hip flexor length."
